zh:
《山海经》是先秦传下的一部奇书，约三万余字，成书于战国至西汉之间，作者不详，旧题大禹、伯益所记。今本十八卷经西汉刘歆校定，东晋郭璞作注并撰图赞，分《山经》五卷与《海经》十三卷：《山经》以南、西、北、东、中五方为纲，一列一列地巡行群山；《海经》则记海外、海内与大荒的远国异人。

它的笔法是账簿式的白描：某山在某山之东若干里，山上多什么玉石草木，水从哪里发源、注入哪条河，然后才轮到怪物出场——状如什么、叫声像什么、吃了或佩了有什么用、一旦现身预示什么灾祥。九尾狐、烛龙、刑天、精卫都以这样干燥的句式初次登场。山川、物产、巫术、祭礼混编在一起，与其说是神话集，不如说是一部想象世界的博物志与祭祀地理，鲁迅在《中国小说史略》里径称之为"古之巫书"。

正因为只给骨架不给血肉，《山海经》成了后世取之不尽的素材库：陶渊明"流观山海图"，志怪小说、明清图本、今天的游戏与影视都从这些三言两语里生发出完整的形象。读它最好的方式，仍是像上古的行旅一样一山一山走过去，看每座山交出什么怪物、什么药方、什么山神。
en:
The Shanhaijing, or Classic of Mountains and Seas, is the strangest book to survive from early China: some thirty thousand characters compiled between the Warring States period and the Western Han, author unknown, traditionally credited to Yu the Great and his minister Bo Yi. The received text of eighteen chapters was collated by Liu Xin in the Western Han and annotated by Guo Pu in the fourth century CE. Five chapters of 'Mountain Classics' survey the peaks of the south, west, north, east, and center range by range; thirteen 'Sea Classics' describe the far lands and peoples beyond the seas and in the great wilderness.

Its manner is that of a ledger. Each entry fixes a mountain so many li east of the last, lists its jade, plants, and metals, traces where its river rises and debouches — and only then admits the monster: shaped like such-and-such, voiced like something else, useful to eat or to wear, and portending flood, drought, fire, or peace when seen. The nine-tailed fox, the torch dragon Zhulong, the headless warrior Xingtian, and the sea-filling bird Jingwei all make their first appearance in this dry syntax. Geography, natural history, magic, and sacrificial protocol are filed together; Lu Xun called the book 'the shamans' scripture of antiquity'.

Because it gives skeletons rather than stories, the Shanhaijing became an inexhaustible quarry. Tao Yuanming browsed its illustrated charts for pleasure; the tale literature, the Ming and Qing picture editions, and today's games and films have all grown full bodies from its two-line creatures. The best way to read it is still the oldest: walk the ranges one mountain at a time, and see what beast, what remedy, what god each peak surrenders.
